title: Reading and Use of English – Part 5
description: You are going to read a magazine article about an intellectual process known as critical thinking. For questions 31 – 36, choose the answer (A, B, C or D) which you think fits best according to the text. Mark your answers on the separate answer sheet.

content.title: The Science of Habit: Rewiring the Routine
content.subtitle: How understanding the 'habit loop' can help us master our subconscious behaviors.
content.text: For most of our waking lives, we are not making conscious decisions. From the way we tie our shoes to the route we drive to work, up to 40% of our daily activities are governed by habit—automated routines stored in the basal ganglia, a primitive part of the brain. This neurological efficiency is a survival mechanism, allowing the prefrontal cortex to focus on more complex tasks while the rest of the brain operates on autopilot. [31] However, the brain does not distinguish between good habits and bad ones; once a routine is encoded, it becomes a permanent neural pathway, waiting for the right trigger to be activated. [/31] This is why 'breaking' a habit is so difficult; you cannot simply delete the neural path, you can only pave over it with a stronger one. || At the heart of every habit is a three-step 'habit loop': the cue, the routine, and the reward. The cue is a trigger that tells your brain to go into automatic mode. [32] The reward is the physical or emotional 'hit'—often a release of dopamine—that tells your brain that this specific loop is worth remembering for the future. [/32] Most people focus on the routine itself (e.g., eating junk food), but psychologists argue that to change a behavior, one must first identify the underlying craving that the reward satisfies. If you eat a cookie every afternoon at 3:00 PM, is it because you are hungry, or because you need a social break from your desk? Without identifying the true reward, any attempt at change is likely to fail. || [33] The writer’s tone when discussing the 'habit-forming' design of modern technology is one of profound wariness. [/33] App developers and interface designers are increasingly using 'persuasive technology'—design patterns that exploit the habit loop to keep users engaged for as long as possible. The 'pull-to-refresh' mechanism on social media feeds, for instance, mimics the psychological pull of a slot machine. We are not just using these tools; we are being conditioned by them. This environmental engineering makes it incredibly difficult for the average individual to maintain 'cognitive sovereignty' over their own time and attention. || One effective strategy for change is 'habit stacking,' a concept popularized by BJ Fogg and James Clear. [34] This involves anchoring a new, desired behavior to an existing, well-established habit. [/34] For example, if you want to start a daily meditation practice, you might commit to doing it immediately after your morning cup of coffee. By piggybacking on an existing neural pathway, you bypass the need for intense willpower, which is a finite and easily exhausted resource. It is a way of 'hacking' the brain’s desire for efficiency to work in your favor. || [35] However, some researchers caution that this focus on individual 'self-optimization' can overlook the structural factors that influence behavior. [/35] It is much harder to build healthy habits in a 'food desert' or in a workplace culture that rewards burnout. The narrative of 'personal responsibility' can sometimes be used to deflect blame from companies that profit from our bad habits. While understanding the habit loop is a powerful tool for personal change, it is not a substitute for environmental or policy-level interventions that make the 'healthy choice the easy choice.' || Ultimately, mastering our habits is about reclaiming the 40% of our lives that currently happens without our consent. [36] The writer concludes that while we may never be able to fully erase our past routines, we have the capacity to choose which loops we will feed and which we will allow to starve. [/36] It is a slow, iterative process of awareness and adjustment. By becoming the architects of our own routines, we move from being passive passengers in our own minds to being active pilots of our own destiny. The first step is not a leap of will, but a moment of observation.

questions.question: In the first paragraph, the writer suggests that habits are difficult to break because...
options: A) the prefrontal cortex is not involved in the habit-making process.
B) tieing one's shoes is a more complex task than we realize.
C) the basal ganglia is more powerful than the rest of the human brain.
D) the brain cannot differentiate between beneficial and harmful routines.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [31], which states the brain 'does not distinguish between good habits and bad ones.' A is incorrect as the prefrontal cortex is simply freed up by habits, not excluded. C is not mentioned. D is a minor example, not the reason for the difficulty.

questions.question: What is the function of the 'reward' in the habit loop?
options: A) It eliminates the need for any kind of cue in the next cycle.
B) It allows the prefrontal cortex to take control of the behavior.
C) It provides a signal that the routine should be repeated in the future.
D) It acts as a trigger to start the automatic routine.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [32] and the mention of the reward telling the brain the loop is 'worth remembering.' A describes the 'cue.' C is the opposite of how habits work. D is incorrect as the cue remains necessary.

questions.question: What is the author's attitude toward 'persuasive technology' in the third paragraph?
options: A) He is neutral, viewing it as a natural evolution of marketing.
B) He is optimistic that it can be used to promote healthy habits.
C) He is concerned that it undermines our control over our own minds.
D) He is impressed by the creativity of modern app developers.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [33] and the mention of 'profound wariness' and the loss of 'cognitive sovereignty.' A and D are not supported by the tone. B is too mild.

questions.question: How does 'habit stacking' help in creating new behaviors?
options: A) It uses willpower to force a new routine into the brain.
B) It connects a new goal to a routine that the brain already performs automatically.
C) It involves doing several new tasks at the same time to save energy.
D) It requires a person to stop all their old habits before starting new ones.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [34] and the idea of 'anchoring' a new behavior to an 'existing, well-established habit.' A is incorrect as the text says it 'bypasses the need for intense willpower.' B and D are not part of the 'habit stacking' concept.

questions.question: What does the author suggest about 'self-optimization' in the fifth paragraph?
options: A) It is often used as a way for corporations to avoid their own responsibilities.
B) It is the only effective way to deal with bad habits in the workplace.
C) It is more important than environmental or policy changes.
D) It is impossible to achieve without a deep understanding of neuroscience.
questions.explanation: B is supported by [35] and the mention of 'deflect blame from companies that profit from our bad habits.' A and D are contradicted by the author's call for 'policy-level interventions.' C is not mentioned.

questions.question: Which of the following best describes the writer's conclusion in the final paragraph?
options: A) Awareness is a more powerful tool for change than willpower alone.
B) We have the power to consciously choose which habits to reinforce.
C) Modern technology has made it impossible to be the 'pilots of our destiny'.
D) We are destined to repeat our old habits regardless of our efforts.
questions.explanation: C is supported by [36] and the idea that we 'choose which loops we will feed.' A is too pessimistic. B is a theme, but C is the specific conclusion about 'feeding' or 'starving' loops. D is an overstatement of the author's warnings.
